docs.title: Welcome
docs.text:
Llama is a Grasshopper plugin for finite element analysis using CalculiX. It lets you model, mesh, and analyse structures with beam, shell, and solid elements — all within a parametric Grasshopper workflow.

Llama is developed by Marco Pellegrino.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Meshing – Generate tetrahedral meshes via Gmsh directly in Grasshopper
  • Materials – Isotropic, orthotropic, spring, and stiffness matrix materials
  • Elements – Tetra, hexa, and shell elements
  • Analysis – Linear static, nonlinear static, dynamic implicit, and frequency steps
  • Post-processing – Read CalculiX results and visualise deformed shapes and stresses

docs.title: Getting started
docs.text:
Head to Installation to set up Llama, then explore the Basics to learn how each component works.

Note

The author cannot be held responsible for the output. Results should always be validated against another finite element analysis package.

docs.title: Buy a License
docs.text: Llama offers a free tier for models with up to 100 elements. For larger models, a license is required.

docs.title: Academic License — Free
docs.text:
For students, researchers, and educators. Valid for 1 year, renewable upon request.

  • University coursework and teaching
  • Academic research and publications
  • Personal learning and experimentation

Not for commercial use

The Academic License does not cover professional or commercial work. See the License terms for details.

To request an Academic License, send an email to llama.calculix@gmail.com with:

  • Your full name
  • University or institution name
  • Academic email address

You will receive a license file (data.bin) within 48 hours.

docs.title: Commercial License — 47 € + VAT / year
docs.text:
For engineering firms, consultancies, freelancers, and any commercial use.

  • Per-seat license, valid for 1 year
  • Access to all versions released within the maintenance period
  • Email support with a maximum 48-hour response time

docs.title: After Payment
docs.text:
Once payment is confirmed, you will receive a license file (data.bin) by email within 48 hours. To activate it:

  1. Open Grasshopper
  2. Go to Llama → License → Activate License
  3. Select the data.bin file you received

docs.text: If you require a quotation, proforma invoice, or your company's details on the invoice, email llama.calculix@gmail.com before paying. Otherwise, you will receive an invoice with the name and email address associated with your payment.

docs.title: Contact
docs.text: For any licensing questions: llama.calculix@gmail.com

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: Grasshopper is a visual programming language for Rhino3D, a popular 3D modelling software. You can install Llama using the Package Manager.

docs.title: Package Manager
docs.text:
1. Open Rhino3D and launch the Package Manager from the Tools menu — or click the link.

  2. Search for Llama and click Install. The Package Manager will download and install the plugin.

  3. Restart Rhino3D to activate the plugin.

Note

If you have correctly followed the steps above, you will find the Llama tab on the Grasshopper toolbar.

docs.title: Install CalculiX
docs.text:
Llama requires a CalculiX (ccx) executable to run analyses.

  1. Download CalculiX from calculix.de or use a prebuilt binary such as bConverged.
  2. Install or extract the executable on your machine.

Note

Llama will automatically try to detect the CalculiX executable on your system. If it cannot be found, you can manually set the path in the Run with Exe component.

docs.title: Basics
docs.text:
Core concepts and components to build, solve, and inspect models in Llama.

Analysis The **Build Input Deck** component writes the assembled model to a CalculiX `.inp` file. Assemble Model The **CcxModel** component assembles all parts of the structural model into a single CalculiX model ready for analysis. Boundary Conditions The **Support** component applies fixed boundary conditions to selected nodes, constraining all translational and rot... Elements Llama supports the following CalculiX solid element types: Loads Applies a gravitational body force to the entire model. Materials Llama supports several material models for use with CalculiX. Meshing Llama integrates Gmsh for tetrahedral mesh generation. Results The **Read Results** component parses CalculiX output files (`.dat`, `.frd`) and extracts analysis results. Analysis Steps Steps define the type of analysis to perform.

docs.title: Build Input Deck
docs.text: The Build Input Deck component writes the assembled model to a CalculiX .inp file.

docs.title: Run Analysis
docs.text:
The Run with Exe component executes CalculiX on the generated input deck.

Parameter Description Input Deck Path Path to the .inp file CalculiX Path Path to the ccx executable

Note

Ensure CalculiX is installed and the path is correct before running.

docs.title: Assemble Model
docs.text: The CcxModel component assembles all parts of the structural model into a single CalculiX model ready for analysis.

docs.title: Inputs
docs.text: Input Description Mesh / Model The meshed structural model Materials Material definitions Boundary Conditions Supports and constraints Loads Applied loads Steps Analysis steps with output requests

docs.title: Output
docs.text: A complete CalculiX model object that can be passed to the Build Input Deck or Run Analysis components.

docs.title: Fixed Support
docs.text:
The Support component applies fixed boundary conditions to selected nodes, constraining all translational and rotational degrees of freedom.

Select nodes by point location or by node set.

docs.title: Elements
docs.text: Llama supports the following CalculiX solid element types:

docs.title: Tetrahedral Elements
docs.text:
Element Nodes Description Tetra4 4 Linear tetrahedron Tetra10 10 Quadratic tetrahedron

Tetrahedral elements are generated automatically by the Gmsh Tetra Mesh component. Use linear (4-node) elements for fast preliminary analyses and quadratic (10-node) elements for higher accuracy.

docs.title: Hexahedral Elements
docs.text:
Element Nodes Description Hexa20 20 Quadratic hexahedron (brick)

Hexahedral meshes can be imported from external tools and converted using the Hex Mesh to Model component.

docs.title: Gravity Load
docs.text:
Applies a gravitational body force to the entire model.

Parameter Description Direction Gravity vector (e.g. 0, 0, -1) Magnitude Acceleration (e.g. 9.81)

docs.title: Nodal Load
docs.text:
Applies a concentrated force or moment at selected nodes.

Parameter Description Node Target node(s) Force Force vector (Fx, Fy, Fz)

docs.title: Materials
docs.text: Llama supports several material models for use with CalculiX.

docs.title: Isotropic Material
docs.text:
A linear elastic isotropic material defined by:

Parameter Description Young's Modulus Elastic modulus (E) Poisson's Ratio Poisson's ratio (ν) Density Mass density (ρ)

docs.title: Orthotropic Material
docs.text: An orthotropic material with independent properties in three directions.

docs.title: Spring Material
docs.text: A spring-type material for point-to-point spring elements.

docs.title: Stiffness Matrix Material
docs.text:
A general stiffness matrix material for advanced element formulations.

Note

For most structural analyses, the Isotropic Material component is sufficient.

docs.title: Meshing
docs.text: Llama integrates Gmsh for tetrahedral mesh generation.

docs.title: Tetra Mesh
docs.text:
The Gmsh Tetra Mesh component generates a tetrahedral mesh from a geometry file. It supports STEP, IGES, and STL input formats. STEP and IGES files are processed with the OpenCASCADE geometry kernel, while STL files use Gmsh's built-in kernel.

Llama automatically locates the Gmsh executable on your system. If it cannot be found, you can provide the path manually.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Input Description Geometry File Path to the geometry file (.step, .iges, .stl) Min Size Minimum characteristic element length Max Size Maximum characteristic element length Element Order Element order (1 = linear, 2 = quadratic) Algorithm 3D 3D meshing algorithm Optimize Enable mesh optimization Optimize Netgen Enable Netgen optimization Smoothing Number of smoothing steps High Order Optimize Optimization level for high-order elements

docs.title: Output
docs.text: A tetrahedral mesh that can be converted to a Llama structural model using the Tetra Mesh to Model component.

docs.title: Hex Mesh
docs.text: Hexahedral meshes can be imported from external tools and converted using the Hex Mesh to Model component.

docs.title: Read Results
docs.text: The Read Results component parses CalculiX output files (.dat, .frd) and extracts analysis results.

docs.title: Visualisation
docs.text:
The Vis Results component displays deformed shapes and stress contours in the Rhino viewport.

Results include:

  • Displacements – nodal translations
  • Stresses – element/nodal stress tensors (averaged at nodes)
  • Reaction forces – support reactions

docs.title: Analysis Steps
docs.text: Steps define the type of analysis to perform.

docs.title: Linear Static
docs.text: A single-step linear elastic analysis. The most common step type for basic structural problems.

docs.title: Nonlinear Static
docs.text: An incremental nonlinear static analysis with load stepping and convergence control.

docs.title: Output Requests
docs.text: Each step can include output requests for nodal and element results (displacements, stresses, reaction forces, etc.).

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Choose an example workflow:

Analysis This example focuses on setting up and running structural analysis with Llama. Generate Geometry This example shows how to create clean analysis-ready geometry before meshing. Optimisation This example demonstrates a simple optimization loop driven by Grasshopper. Post Processing This example covers extracting and visualizing results after analysis.

docs.title: Analysis
docs.text: This example focuses on setting up and running structural analysis with Llama.

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
1. Define materials and sections
  2. Apply boundary conditions and loads
  3. Configure analysis steps
  4. Assemble model and run CalculiX
  5. Review convergence and outputs

Note

Add screenshots and downloadable definitions to make this tutorial fully reproducible.

docs.title: Generate Geometry
docs.text: This example shows how to create clean analysis-ready geometry before meshing.

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
1. Build or import geometry in Rhino
  2. Clean and simplify curves/surfaces/solids
  3. Organize model layers for supports, loads, and parts
  4. Prepare geometry for meshing and section assignment

Note

Add screenshots and downloadable definitions to make this tutorial fully reproducible.

docs.title: Optimisation
docs.text: This example demonstrates a simple optimization loop driven by Grasshopper.

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
1. Define design variables
  2. Generate model variants
  3. Run analysis for each variant
  4. Evaluate objective and constraints
  5. Select best-performing solution

Note

Add screenshots and downloadable definitions to make this tutorial fully reproducible.

docs.title: Post Processing
docs.text: This example covers extracting and visualizing results after analysis.

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
1. Read displacements, stresses, and reactions
  2. Display contour plots and deformed shapes
  3. Compare key values at selected points
  4. Export plots and tables for reporting

Note

Add screenshots and downloadable definitions to make this tutorial fully reproducible.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
Reference material for solver details, licensing, and purchase.

CalculiX CalculiX is a free, open-source finite element solver developed by Guido Dhondt and Klaus Wittig. License Llama is developed by the Pellegrino Research Group.

docs.title: CalculiX
docs.text:
CalculiX is a free, open-source finite element solver developed by Guido Dhondt and Klaus Wittig.

Llama uses CalculiX as its analysis backend. The input deck format (.inp) is compatible with the CalculiX CrunchiX (ccx) solver.

docs.title: Useful links
docs.text:
• CalculiX homepage
  • CalculiX documentation (PDF)
  • bConverged prebuilt binaries

docs.title: License
docs.text: Llama is developed by the Pellegrino Research Group.

docs.title: Dual License Model
docs.text: Llama is available under two license types:

docs.title: Academic License
docs.text:
The Academic License is granted free of charge to students, researchers, and educators for non-commercial use only. This includes:

  • University coursework and teaching
  • Academic research and publications
  • Personal learning and experimentation

The Academic License does not permit:

  • Use in professional or commercial engineering projects
  • Use by or on behalf of a company, consultancy, or any for-profit entity
  • Use in work that generates revenue, directly or indirectly
  • Redistribution of the software or any derivative works

Academic licenses are issued with an expiry date and must be renewed periodically. To request an Academic License, contact us at the address below.

docs.title: Commercial License
docs.text:
A Commercial License is required for any use not covered by the Academic License. This includes use by engineering firms, consultancies, freelancers, and any entity using Llama in projects that generate revenue.

Commercial licenses are available per seat. For pricing and purchase, visit llama-ccx.github.io or contact us directly.

docs.title: Free Tier
docs.text: Llama may be used without a license for models with up to 100 elements. Beyond this limit, a valid Academic or Commercial license is required.
